ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for chilli garlic stir-fried prawns specifically; FOODON_03302051 (shrimp food product) used as the closest parent anchor. No exact term for this Indo-Chinese preparation exists in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked shrimps/prawns as a category; no specific stir-fried chilli garlic preparation code exists in FoodEx2. Used as the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to chilli garlic stir-fried prawns; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Chilli Garlic Prawns is approximately 250 g (one plate), providing around 380 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, cooked (FDC ID 175180) and HPB Singapore Foodo — Stir-fried prawn dishes (composite adaptation)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Chilli Garlic Prawns' exists in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180 (Shrimp, cooked) as the primary approved reference for the prawn component, cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo stir-fried prawn dish composites. Input values from the ai_generated GrabFood record (confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with these approved sources. Sauce-component contributions (sodium, sugar, carbohydrate) are consistent with HPB Foodo stir-fried seafood with sauce entries. No material discrepancies found for core macros.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chilli Garlic Prawns in published literature. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous stir-fried seafood dishes with sauce components referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The dish is protein-dominant (12 g/100 g) with low carbohydrate (6 g/100 g) from sauce ingredients (ketchup, chilli sauce). GL per serving calculated as: GI (25) × carbohydrate per serving (15 g from 250 g × 6 g/100 g) / 100 = 3.75, rounded to 3.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg/100 g — exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; driven by soy sauce, chilli sauce, and tomato ketchup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce to bring sodium below 300 mg/100 g; avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6 g/100 g) and low sugar (3.2 g/100 g) with high protein (12 g/100 g) from prawns supports a low glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chilli Garlic Prawns is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without white rice; request reduced sauce quantities to minimise added sugars from ketchup and chilli sauce, and monitor total carbohydrate intake from accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (480 mg/100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 1.4 g/100 g and trans fat is 0 g/100 g, which are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and zero trans fat profile is favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content (480 mg/100 g) requires portion control; individuals with CVD should request reduced-sauce preparation and limit serving to 150 g to keep sodium intake within meal targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Prawns provide lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory potential; however, red chilli and tomato are nightshade vegetables that may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of garlic and prawn-derived omega-3s, but should monitor for symptom flares related to nightshade ingredients (chilli, tomato) and request reduced sauce to lower sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Garlic Prawns is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion dish served in Singapore, featuring succulent whole prawns stir-fried at high heat in a bold, aromatic sauce made from fresh red chilli, minced garlic, spring onion, soy sauce, chilli sauce, and tomato ketchup. The cooking method is wok stir-frying, which imparts a characteristic wok hei (breath of the wok) flavour while preserving the natural sweetness and firm texture of the prawns. The dish delivers a spicy, savoury, and slightly tangy flavour profile with a glossy, clingy sauce. Nutritionally, it provides 152 kcal per 100 g, with 12 g protein, 8.8 g fat (of which only 1.4 g is saturated), and 6 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-rich, relatively low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 480 mg/100 g due to the sauce components. The dish is halal-certified in many Singapore restaurants and is commonly served as a shared main course with steamed rice or as a standalone protein dish. It is clinically suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycaemic load, but requires sodium moderation for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The garlic and prawn components offer anti-inflammatory micronutrients relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.

confidence_reason: Input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood (a forbidden primary nutrient source). Nutrient values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180, Shrimp cooked) and HPB Singapore Foodo stir-fried prawn composites and found to be broadly consistent. Confidence is set to medium because no exact entry for this specific dish preparation exists in any approved source; values are adapted from the closest approved-source equivalents.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific Indo-Chinese stir-fried prawn preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180) or HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2024. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): stir-fried preparation with vegetable oil does not generate trans fats; consistent with USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data (FDC ID 175180). 3. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 25, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database stir-fried seafood analogues per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in published literature. 4. The input cultural.primary_cuisine field referenced specific restaurant names ('Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine') which are not appropriate cuisine descriptors; corrected to 'Indo-Chinese / Singaporean' based on dish description and cooking style. 5. Nutrient values from the ai_generated GrabFood input were retained after cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central and HPB Foodo; no material discrepancies found for core macros. 6. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact codes exist for this specific preparation; gap notes document the limitation.
